sentences.chinese: 武曰：「本無謀，又非親屬，何謂相坐？」
sentences.literal: Wu said, "Originally there was no plot, and moreover I am not kin—what is meant by sharing punishment?"
sentences.idiomatic: Su Wu replied, "I had no part in the plot and I am not Zhang Sheng's kinsman—why should I share his guilt?"

sentences.chinese: 復舉劍擬之，武不動。
sentences.literal: Again he raised the sword as if to strike him; Wu did not move.
sentences.idiomatic: Wei Lu raised the blade again; Su Wu did not flinch.

sentences.chinese: 律曰：「蘇君，律前負漢歸匈奴，幸蒙大恩，賜號稱王，擁眾數萬，馬畜彌山，富貴如此。
sentences.literal: Lu said, "Lord Su, Lu formerly betrayed the Han and returned to the Xiongnu; fortunately receiving great favor, I was granted a title and called king, holding a host of several tens of thousands, horses and herds filling the mountains—wealth and honor such as this.
sentences.idiomatic: "Lord Su," said Wei Lu, "I once deserted Han for the steppe and was rewarded with a king's title, tens of thousands of followers, and herds that covered the hills—such is the fortune of those who yield."

sentences.chinese: 蘇君今日降，明日復然。
sentences.literal: Lord Su, if you surrender today, tomorrow will again be thus.
sentences.idiomatic: Surrender today, and tomorrow you will stand where I stand."

sentences.chinese: 空以身膏草野，誰復知之！」
sentences.literal: In vain you make your body grease the grassland—who will again know you!"
sentences.idiomatic: Or you may fatten the steppe with your bones—and who will remember your name?"

sentences.chinese: 武不應。
sentences.literal: Wu did not respond.
sentences.idiomatic: Su Wu was silent.

sentences.chinese: 律曰：「君因我降，與君為兄弟，今不聽吾計，後雖欲復見我，尚可得乎？」
sentences.literal: Lu said, "If you, lord, surrender through me, I will be brothers with you; now if you do not heed my plan, afterward even if you wish again to see me, can it still be obtained?"
sentences.idiomatic: Wei Lu pressed him: "Yield through my good offices and we shall be as brothers; refuse now, and you will never look on my face again."

sentences.chinese: 武罵律曰：「女為人臣子，不顧恩義，畔主背親，為降虜於蠻夷，何以女為見？
sentences.literal: Wu reviled Lu, saying, "You as man's subject and son do not heed grace and rightness, rebel against the ruler and betray kin, become a surrendered captive among the barbarians—why should I see you?
sentences.idiomatic: Su Wu railed at him: "You were a subject and a son—faithless to ruler and family, a traitor groveling among barbarians. Why should I dignify you with my gaze?

sentences.chinese: 且單于信女，使決人死生，不平心持正，反欲斗兩主，觀禍敗。
sentences.literal: Moreover the Chanyu trusts you, orders you to decide men's life and death; you do not level your heart and uphold rectitude, but instead wish to make the two rulers fight and watch disaster and defeat.
sentences.idiomatic: The Chanyu trusts you with men's lives, yet instead of judging fairly you would set our two sovereigns at each other's throats and gloat over the ruin.

sentences.chinese: 南越殺漢使者，屠為九郡；
sentences.literal: Southern Yue killed Han envoys and was slaughtered into nine commanderies;
sentences.idiomatic: Southern Yue murdered Han envoys and was carved into nine commanderies;

sentences.chinese: 宛王殺漢使者，頭縣北闕；
sentences.literal: the king of Yuan killed Han envoys—his head hung at the northern gate tower;
sentences.idiomatic: the king of Dayuan killed Han envoys—his head hung over the Changle gate;

sentences.chinese: 朝鮮殺漢使者，即時誅滅。
sentences.literal: Chaoxian killed Han envoys and was at once exterminated.
sentences.idiomatic: Korea slew Han envoys and was wiped out overnight.

sentences.chinese: 獨匈奴未耳。
sentences.literal: Only the Xiongnu have not yet.
sentences.idiomatic: Only the Xiongnu have not yet paid that price.

sentences.chinese: 若知我不降明，欲令兩國相攻，匈奴之禍從我始矣。」
sentences.literal: You know I will not surrender—that is plain; if you wish thereby to set the two states at war, the Xiongnu's calamity will begin with me."
sentences.idiomatic: You know I will never yield; use that to goad our two realms into war, and the Xiongnu will rue the day they began with me."

sentences.chinese: 律知武終不可脅，白單于。
sentences.literal: Lu knew Wu in the end could not be coerced and reported to the Chanyu.
sentences.idiomatic: Seeing that Su Wu would not break, Wei Lu reported to the Chanyu.

sentences.chinese: 單于愈益欲降之，乃幽武置大窖中，絕不飲食。
sentences.literal: The Chanyu all the more wished to make him surrender; then he imprisoned Wu, placing him in a great cellar, cutting off all drink and food.
sentences.idiomatic: The Chanyu was more determined than ever to break him and shut him in a dry cellar without food or water.

sentences.chinese: 天雨雪，武臥嚙雪與旃毛並咽之，數日不死。
sentences.literal: Heaven rained snow; Wu lay chewing snow together with felt wool and swallowing them; several days he did not die.
sentences.idiomatic: Snow fell; he chewed snow and felt from his cloak and lived for days.

sentences.chinese: 匈奴以為神，乃徙武北海上無人處，使牧羝，羝乳乃得歸。
sentences.literal: The Xiongnu thought him divine; then they moved Wu to the uninhabited region of the northern sea, ordering him to herd rams—when the rams give milk he may return.
sentences.idiomatic: They took him for something more than human and banished him to the shores of the Northern Sea to herd rams, saying he could go home only when the wethers gave milk.

sentences.chinese: 別其官屬常惠等，各置他所。
sentences.literal: He separated his official subordinates Chang Hui and others, each placing them elsewhere.
sentences.idiomatic: His officers, including Chang Hui, were scattered to separate posts.

sentences.chinese: 武既至海上，廩食不至，掘野鼠去草實而食之。
sentences.literal: When Wu had reached the sea, official rations did not arrive; he dug wild mice and removed grass seeds and ate them.
sentences.idiomatic: On the coast no imperial grain reached him; he dug field mice from their stores and ate weed seeds.

sentences.chinese: 杖漢節牧羊，臥起操持，節旄盡落。
sentences.literal: Staff in hand with the Han credentials he herded sheep, lying and rising grasping them—the credential tassels all fell off.
sentences.idiomatic: He leaned on the Han staff as he tended the flock, waking and sleeping with it in his hand until every tuft of silk had worn away.

sentences.chinese: 積五、六年，單于弟於靬王弋射海上。
sentences.literal: After accumulating five or six years, the Chanyu's younger brother the King of Yujian hunted with bow on the sea.
sentences.idiomatic: Five or six years passed; the Chanyu's younger brother, the King of Yujian, came hunting along the shore.

sentences.chinese: 武能網紡繳，檠弓弩，於靬王愛之，給其衣食。
sentences.literal: Wu could net and spin cord, adjust crossbows; the King of Yujian loved him and supplied his clothing and food.
sentences.idiomatic: Su Wu could weave nets and bowstrings and tune crossbows; the king took a liking to him and saw that he was fed and clothed.

sentences.chinese: 三歲餘，王病，賜武馬畜、服匿、穹廬。
sentences.literal: After more than three years the king fell ill and bestowed on Wu horses and herds, felt boots, and a felt yurt.
sentences.idiomatic: More than three years later the king fell ill and gave him mounts, herds, felt boots, and a yurt.

sentences.chinese: 王死後，人眾徙去。
sentences.literal: After the king died, the people and host moved away.
sentences.idiomatic: When the king died, his people struck camp and left.

sentences.chinese: 其冬，丁令盜武牛羊，武復窮厄。
sentences.literal: That winter the Dingling stole Wu's oxen and sheep; Wu again met distress and hardship.
sentences.idiomatic: That winter Dingling raiders drove off his flocks and he was destitute again.

sentences.chinese: 初，武與李陵俱為侍中，武使匈奴明年，陵降，不敢求武。
sentences.literal: In the beginning Wu and Li Ling were both palace attendants; the year after Wu was sent to the Xiongnu Ling surrendered and did not dare seek Wu.
sentences.idiomatic: Su Wu and Li Ling had once served together as palace attendants; the year after Su Wu's mission Li Ling surrendered and was too ashamed to visit him.

sentences.chinese: 久之，單于使陵至海上，為武置酒設樂，因謂武曰：「單于聞陵與子卿素厚，故使陵來說足下，虛心欲相待。
sentences.literal: After a long time the Chanyu ordered Ling to go to the sea; Ling for Wu set out wine and arranged music, and thereupon said to Wu, "The Chanyu has heard that Ling and Ziqing have long been close, therefore sent Ling to persuade you, with empty heart wishing to treat you well.
sentences.idiomatic: In time the Chanyu sent Li Ling to the Northern Sea with wine and music for Su Wu and said through him, "He knows we were old friends and bids me invite you in earnest—he means to honor you if you yield."

sentences.chinese: 終不得歸漢，空自苦亡人之地，信義安所見乎？
sentences.literal: In the end you cannot return to the Han; in vain you torment yourself in a land of exiles—where is faith and right to be seen?
sentences.idiomatic: You will never see Chang'an again; you waste your strength in exile—who will know your loyalty?

sentences.chinese: 前長君為奉車，從至雍棫陽宮，扶輦下除，觸柱折轅，劾大不敬，伏劍自刎，賜錢二百萬以葬。
sentences.literal: Formerly Lord Changjun was chariot attendant; following to Yong's Yuyang Palace, he supported the carriage down the steps, struck a pillar and broke the shaft, was impeached for great irreverence, fell on his sword and cut his own throat, and was granted two million cash for burial.
sentences.idiomatic: Your elder brother once served as chariot attendant; at the Yuyang Palace in Yong he guided the imperial carriage from the steps, snapped an axle against a pillar, was charged with lèse-majesté, and opened his own throat—the court gave two million cash for his funeral.

sentences.chinese: 孺卿從祠河東后土，宦騎與黃門駙馬爭船，推墮駙馬河中溺死，宦騎亡，詔使孺卿逐捕不得，惶恐飲藥而死。
sentences.literal: Ruqing followed the sacrifice to Hou-earth east of the river; a mounted eunuch and a Yellow Gate groom disputed a boat, pushed and dropped the groom into the river where he drowned; the mounted eunuch fled; an edict ordered Ruqing to pursue and capture but he could not; in fear and trembling he drank poison and died.
sentences.idiomatic: Your younger brother Ruqing escorted the sacrifice at Hedong; a palace rider quarreled with a groom over a boat, knocked the groom into the river, and fled; the emperor ordered your brother to run the man down. When he failed, he took poison in terror.

sentences.chinese: 來時，大夫人已不幸，陵送葬至陽陵。
sentences.literal: When I came, the great lady had already met misfortune; Ling escorted the funeral to Yangling.
sentences.idiomatic: When I left for the steppe your mother had already died; I walked her hearse to Yangling myself.

sentences.chinese: 子卿婦年少，聞已更嫁矣。
sentences.literal: Ziqing's wife is young; I have heard she has already remarried.
sentences.idiomatic: Your wife was young—I hear she has married again.

sentences.chinese: 獨有女弟二人，兩女一男，今復十餘年，存亡不可知。
sentences.literal: You have only two younger sisters, two daughters and one son; now again more than ten years—life or death cannot be known.
sentences.idiomatic: You left two sisters behind and three young children—two girls and a boy. Another decade has passed; who knows if any of them still live?

sentences.chinese: 人生如朝露，何久自苦如此！
sentences.literal: Human life is like morning dew—why long torment yourself thus!
sentences.idiomatic: A man's life is morning dew—why torture yourself year after year?

sentences.chinese: 陵始降時，忽忽如狂，自痛負漢，加以老母系保宮，子卿不欲降，何以過陵？
sentences.literal: When Ling first surrendered, I was dazed as if mad, myself grieved at betraying the Han, and moreover my old mother was bound in the Guard Palace—if Ziqing does not wish to surrender, how can you surpass Ling?
sentences.idiomatic: When I first yielded I was half mad with shame for Han; my mother was locked in the Guard Palace. If you will not bend, you outdo even me in stubbornness.

sentences.chinese: 且陛下春秋高，法令亡常，大臣亡罪夷滅者數十家，安危不可知，子卿尚復誰為乎？
sentences.literal: Moreover His Majesty's spring and autumn are high; laws and edicts are without constancy; great ministers without guilt exterminated number several tens of families—safety and danger cannot be known—for whom does Ziqing still act?
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor is old; laws shift overnight; dozens of great houses have been wiped out without cause. Who can say what morning will bring—for whom do you still hold out?

sentences.chinese: 願聽陵計，勿復有雲。」
sentences.literal: I wish you would heed Ling's plan—do not answer with further refusal."
sentences.idiomatic: Hear me out, Ziqing—and do not answer yet."

sentences.chinese: 武曰：「武父子亡功德，皆為陛下所成就，位列將，爵通侯，兄弟親近，常願肝腦塗地。
sentences.literal: Wu said, "Wu father and son lack merit and virtue; all were accomplished by His Majesty—rank listed among generals, nobility reaching full marquis, brothers intimate and close—we constantly wished to smear liver and brains on the ground.
sentences.idiomatic: Su Wu replied, "My father and I had little merit; the throne raised us to general's rank and full marquis, kept my brothers at court—we owed the emperor our lives.

sentences.chinese: 今得殺身自效，雖蒙斧鉞湯鑊，誠甘樂之。
sentences.literal: Now obtaining to kill the body and repay in person—even if I suffer axe and halberd, boiling cauldron, truly I would sweetly delight in it.
sentences.idiomatic: To die now in the emperor's service—even the axe or the cauldron would be joy.

sentences.chinese: 臣事君，猶子事父也。
sentences.literal: The subject serving the ruler is like the son serving the father.
sentences.idiomatic: A subject owes his ruler what a son owes his father.

sentences.chinese: 子為父死亡所恨。
sentences.literal: The son dying for the father—what is there to regret?
sentences.idiomatic: A son who dies for his father has nothing to regret.

sentences.chinese: 願勿復再言。」
sentences.literal: I beg you do not speak again."
sentences.idiomatic: Say no more."

sentences.chinese: 陵與武飲數日，復曰：「子卿壹聽陵言。」
sentences.literal: Ling drank with Wu several days, then said, "Ziqing, for once heed Ling's words."
sentences.idiomatic: They drank together for days before Li Ling tried again: "Ziqing—just this once, hear me out."

sentences.chinese: 武曰：「自分已死久矣！
sentences.literal: Wu said, "I have long judged myself already dead!
sentences.idiomatic: Su Wu said, "I have been a dead man for years!

sentences.chinese: 王必欲降武，請畢今日之歡，效死於前！」
sentences.literal: If the ruler must make Wu surrender, I beg to finish today's merriment and offer death before you!"
sentences.idiomatic: If the Chanyu will have my head before my surrender, let this banquet end—I will die here at once!"

sentences.chinese: 陵見其至誠，喟然歎曰：「嗟乎，義士！
sentences.literal: Ling seeing his utmost sincerity, sighed with a groan, saying, "Alas, man of right!
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling saw he could not be moved and sighed, "Ah—you are a true knight!

sentences.chinese: 陵與衛律之罪上通於天。」
sentences.literal: Ling's and Wei Lu's guilt mounts through to Heaven."
sentences.idiomatic: For Wei Lu and me, our guilt reaches to heaven."

sentences.chinese: 因泣下沾衿，與武決去。
sentences.literal: Thereupon tears fell soaking his collar; with Wu he parted and departed.
sentences.idiomatic: Tears soaked his robe as he took leave of Su Wu.

sentences.chinese: 陵惡自賜武，使其妻賜武牛羊數十頭。
sentences.literal: Ling was ashamed to bestow on Wu himself; he ordered his wife to bestow on Wu several tens of head of oxen and sheep.
sentences.idiomatic: Ashamed to offer gifts in his own name, Li Ling sent his wife with dozens of head of livestock for Su Wu.

sentences.chinese: 後陵復至北海上，語武：「區脫捕得雲中生口，言太守以下吏民皆白服，曰上崩。」
sentences.literal: Later Ling again came to the northern sea and said to Wu, "A border post captured a living mouth from Yunzhong who said that from the governor down clerks and people all wore white mourning, saying the emperor had collapsed."
sentences.idiomatic: Li Ling returned to the Northern Sea and told him: "Frontier scouts took a prisoner from Yunzhong who says every official and commoner wears mourning white—the emperor is dead."

sentences.chinese: 武聞之，南鄉號哭，歐血，旦夕臨數月。
sentences.literal: Wu hearing it, southward wailed and cried, vomited blood, morning and evening mourned several months.
sentences.idiomatic: Su Wu turned south and howled until he coughed blood; for months he mourned morning and night.

sentences.chinese: 武以始元六年春至京師。
sentences.literal: In the spring of the sixth year of Shiyuan Wu reached the capital.
sentences.idiomatic: In the spring of 81 BCE he arrived in Chang'an.

sentences.chinese: 詔武奉一太守謁武帝園廟，拜為典屬國，秩中二千石，賜錢二百萬，公田二頃，宅一區。
sentences.literal: He was edicted to accompany one governor in visiting Emperor Wu's park and temples; he was appointed colonel of dependent states with salary of full two thousand dan, granted two million cash, two qing of public field, and one residence compound.
sentences.idiomatic: The court ordered him to join a governor in sacrifice at Emperor Wu's mausoleum, then named him colonel for dependent states at full two-thousand-dan rank, with two million cash, two qing of land, and a house.

sentences.chinese: 常惠、徐聖、趙終根皆拜為中郎，賜帛各二百匹。
sentences.literal: Chang Hui, Xu Sheng, and Zhao Zhonggen were all appointed gentlemen of the palace and granted two hundred bolts of silk each.
sentences.idiomatic: Chang Hui, Xu Sheng, and Zhao Zhonggen were each made gentleman of the palace with two hundred bolts of silk.

sentences.chinese: 其餘六人老歸家，賜錢人十萬，復終身。
sentences.literal: The remaining six men, old, returned home; each was granted one hundred thousand cash and exemption for life.
sentences.idiomatic: The other six, too old for office, were sent home with a hundred thousand cash apiece and lifetime exemption from labor service.

sentences.chinese: 常惠後至右將軍，封列侯，自有傳。
sentences.literal: Chang Hui later reached general of the right and was enfeoffed full marquis—he has his own biography.
sentences.idiomatic: Chang Hui later rose to general of the right and a full marquisate; he has a separate biography.

sentences.chinese: 武留匈奴凡十九歲，始以強壯出，及還，鬚髮盡白。
sentences.literal: Wu stayed among the Xiongnu in all nineteen years; at first he went out in robust strength; when he returned, beard and hair were all white.
sentences.idiomatic: Nineteen years among the Xiongnu had turned a vigorous envoy into a man of snow-white hair.

sentences.chinese: 武來歸明年，上官桀、子安與桑弘羊及燕王、蓋主謀反。
sentences.literal: The year after Wu came back, Shangguan Jie, his son An, with Sang Hongyang and the Prince of Yan and the Elder Princess of Gai plotted rebellion.
sentences.idiomatic: The year after his return Shangguan Jie, Shangguan An, Sang Hongyang, the Prince of Yan, and Princess Gai plotted a coup.

sentences.chinese: 武子男元與安有謀，坐死。
sentences.literal: Wu's son Yuan had plotted with An; he was condemned to death for complicity.
sentences.idiomatic: Su Wu's son Su Yuan was implicated with Shangguan An and executed.

sentences.chinese: 初，桀、安與大將軍霍光爭權，數疏光過失予燕王，令上書告之。
sentences.literal: In the beginning Jie and An with Supreme Commander Huo Guang struggled for power; repeatedly they spread Guang's faults to the Prince of Yan, ordering him to memorialize and denounce him.
sentences.idiomatic: Earlier, Shangguan Jie and his son had vied with Huo Guang for power, feeding the Prince of Yan memorials listing Huo Guang's supposed crimes.

sentences.chinese: 又言蘇武使匈奴二十年不降，還乃為典屬國，大將軍長史無功勞，為搜粟都尉，光顓權自恣。
sentences.literal: They also said Su Wu was sent to the Xiongnu twenty years without surrendering, yet on return was only made colonel of dependent states; the supreme commander's chief clerk without merit was made grain-search commandant—Guang monopolizes power and indulges himself.
sentences.idiomatic: They charged that Su Wu had endured twenty years without yielding yet received only a colonel's post while Huo Guang's chief clerk, who had done nothing, became grain-search commandant—proof that Huo abused power for his own men.

sentences.chinese: 及燕王等反誅，窮治黨與，武素與桀、弘羊有舊，數為燕王所訟，子又在謀中，廷尉奏請逮捕武。
sentences.literal: When the Prince of Yan and others rebelled and were executed, they exhaustively prosecuted accomplices; Wu had long had old ties with Jie and Hongyang, repeatedly was denounced by the Prince of Yan, and his son was again in the plot—the commandant of justice memorialized requesting Wu's arrest.
sentences.idiomatic: When the Yan faction fell, the purge reached everyone connected; Su Wu had old ties to Jie and Sang Hongyang, had been named in the prince's memorials, and his son had joined the plot—the commandant of justice asked for his arrest.

sentences.chinese: 霍光寢其奏，免武官。
sentences.literal: Huo Guang shelved his memorial and dismissed Wu from office.
sentences.idiomatic: Huo Guang tabled the indictment and merely stripped Su Wu of his post.

sentences.chinese: 數年，昭帝崩，武以故二千石與計謀立宣帝，賜爵關內侯，食邑三百戶。
sentences.literal: Several years later Emperor Zhao died; Wu as a former two-thousand-dan took part in planning to establish Emperor Xuandi and was granted rank as marquis within the passes with three hundred households' food income.
sentences.idiomatic: A few years later, when Emperor Zhao died, Su Wu joined the ministers who enthroned Emperor Xuan and was made marquis within the passes with three hundred households.

sentences.chinese: 久之，衛將軍張安世薦武明習故事，奉使不辱命，先帝以為遺言。
sentences.literal: After a long time General of the Guards Zhang Anshi recommended Wu as clear on precedents, who on mission did not disgrace his charge—the late emperor had taken it as a dying injunction.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhang Anshi later recommended him: Su Wu knew Han ritual inside out and had never failed an embassy—the late emperor had singled him out with his dying words.

sentences.chinese: 宣帝即時召武待詔宦者署，數進見，復為右曹典屬國。
sentences.literal: Emperor Xuandi at once summoned Wu to await edicts at the eunuchs' office, repeatedly advanced him to audience, and again made him colonel of dependent states in the right bureau.
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan recalled him to the eunuch directorate as a waiting official, received him often, and restored him as colonel of dependent states in the right bureau.

sentences.chinese: 以武著節老臣，命朝朔望，號稱祭酒，甚優寵之。
sentences.literal: Because Wu was an old minister of marked integrity, he was ordered to attend court on new and full moons, titled libationer, and was greatly favored and honored.
sentences.idiomatic: Honoring him as an aged minister of unbending principle, the emperor ordered him to attend court on the first and fifteenth of each month, gave him the title of libationer, and treated him with exceptional favor.

sentences.chinese: 武所得賞賜，盡以施予昆弟故人，家不餘財。
sentences.literal: What Wu obtained in rewards and gifts he entirely gave to brothers and old friends; his household had no surplus wealth.
sentences.idiomatic: Every gift and stipend went to kinsmen and old friends; he kept nothing for himself.

sentences.chinese: 皇后父平恩侯、帝舅平昌侯、樂昌侯、車騎將軍韓增、丞相魏相、御史大夫丙吉皆敬重武。
sentences.literal: The empress's father Marquis of Ping'en, the emperor's uncles Marquis of Pingchang and Marquis of Lechang, chariot-and-cavalry general Han Zeng, chancellor Wei Xiang, and imperial counselor Bing Ji all respected and honored Wu.
sentences.idiomatic: The Marquis of Ping'en, the emperor's uncles of Pingchang and Lechang, General Han Zeng, Chancellor Wei Xiang, and Imperial Counselor Bing Ji all held him in the highest regard.

sentences.chinese: 武年老，子前坐事死，上閔之，問左右：「武在匈奴久，豈有子乎？」
sentences.literal: Wu was old; his son had earlier died for a criminal matter; the emperor pitied him and asked those at his sides, "Wu was long among the Xiongnu—could he have had a son?"
sentences.idiomatic: Su Wu was old and his son had been executed; the emperor pitied him and asked his attendants whether he might have left a child on the steppe.

sentences.chinese: 武因平恩侯自白：「前發匈奴時，胡婦適產一子通國，有聲問來，願因使者致金帛贖之。」
sentences.literal: Wu through the Marquis of Ping'en stated himself, saying, "When formerly I was leaving the Xiongnu, a Hu woman had just borne one son Tongguo; word has come—I wish through the envoy to send gold and silks to ransom him."
sentences.idiomatic: Through the Marquis of Ping'en he explained that a Xiongnu wife had borne him a son named Tongguo before he left, and he begged to send gold and silk with the next embassy to buy the boy back.

sentences.chinese: 上許焉。
sentences.literal: The emperor granted it.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.chinese: 後通國隨使者至，上以為郎。
sentences.literal: Afterward Tongguo followed the envoy and arrived; the emperor made him gentleman-attendant.
sentences.idiomatic: Tongguo later came in with an envoy and was appointed gentleman-attendant.

sentences.chinese: 又以武弟子為右曹。
sentences.literal: Moreover he appointed the son of Wu's younger brother to office in the right bureau.
sentences.idiomatic: A nephew of Su Wu was also given a place in the right bureau.

sentences.chinese: 武年八十餘，神爵二年病卒。
sentences.literal: Wu was more than eighty years old; in the second year of Shenjue he died of illness.
sentences.idiomatic: He died of illness in 60 BCE, aged over eighty.

sentences.chinese: 甘露三年，單于始入朝。
sentences.literal: In the third year of Ganlu the Chanyu for the first time entered court.
sentences.idiomatic: In 51 BCE the Chanyu came to Chang'an for the first time.

sentences.chinese: 上思股肱之美，乃圖畫其人於麒麟閣，法其形貌，署其官爵、姓名。
sentences.literal: The emperor thought on the beauty of his thigh-and-arm ministers; then he painted their persons on the Qilin Pavilion, modeled their forms and faces, and inscribed their offices, noble ranks, and names.
sentences.idiomatic: Remembering his greatest ministers, the emperor had their portraits painted in the Qilin Pavilion, true to each man's face, with title and name appended.

sentences.chinese: 唯霍光不名，曰大司馬大將軍博陸侯姓霍氏，次曰衛將軍富平侯張安世，次曰車騎將軍龍額侯韓增，次曰後將軍營平侯趙充國，次曰丞相高平侯魏相，次曰丞相博陽侯丙吉，次曰御史大夫建平侯杜延年，次曰宗正陽城侯劉德，次曰少府梁丘賀，次曰太子太傅蕭望之，次曰典屬國蘇武。
sentences.literal: Only Huo Guang is not named by personal name: it says grand marshal supreme commander Marquis of Bolu, surname Huo; next, general of the guards Marquis of Fuping Zhang Anshi; next, chariot-and-cavalry general Marquis of Long'e Han Zeng; next, general of the rear Marquis of Yingping Zhao Chongguo; next, chancellor Marquis of Gaoping Wei Xiang; next, chancellor Marquis of Boyang Bing Ji; next, imperial counselor Marquis of Jianping Du Yannian; next, director of the imperial clan Marquis of Yangcheng Liu De; next, privy treasurer Liangqiu He; next, grand tutor to the heir apparent Xiao Wangzhi; next, colonel of dependent states Su Wu.
sentences.idiomatic: Only Huo Guang appears by title alone—Grand Marshal, Supreme Commander, Marquis of Bolu, of the house of Huo—followed by Zhang Anshi, Han Zeng, Zhao Chongguo, Wei Xiang, Bing Ji, Du Yannian, Liu De, Liangqiu He, Xiao Wangzhi, and finally Su Wu, colonel of dependent states.

sentences.chinese: 皆有功德，知名當世，是以表而揚之，明著中興輔佐，列於方叔、召虎、仲山甫焉。
sentences.literal: All had merit and virtue, were renowned in their age; therefore they were displayed and exalted, clearly showing the restoration's assistants, ranked with Fang Shu, Zhao Hu, and Zhong Shanfu.
sentences.idiomatic: Each had served the revival of Han with merit and fame; their portraits proclaimed them peers of the Zhou kings' greatest ministers.

sentences.chinese: 凡十一人，皆有傳。
sentences.literal: In all eleven men—all have biographies.
sentences.idiomatic: Eleven men in all—each has his own biography elsewhere.

sentences.chinese: 自丞相黃霸、廷尉於定國、大司農朱邑、京兆尹張敞、右扶風尹翁歸及儒者夏侯勝等，皆以善終，著名宣帝之世，然不得列於名臣之圖，以此知其選矣。
sentences.literal: From Chancellor Huang Ba, commandant of justice Yu Dingguo, grand minister of agriculture Zhu Yi, metropolitan governor Zhang Chang, right superintendent of the capital Yin Wengui, and the scholar Xia Hou Sheng and others—all ended well and were famous in Emperor Xuandi's age, yet could not be listed in the chart of famous ministers; by this one knows the selection.
sentences.idiomatic: Huang Ba, Yu Dingguo, Zhu Yi, Zhang Chang, Yin Wengui, Xia Hou Sheng, and others ended honored careers under Emperor Xuan yet were not painted on that wall—which shows how strict the choice was.

sentences.chinese: 贊曰：李將軍恂恂如鄙人，口不能出辭，及死之日，天下知與不知皆為流涕，彼其中心誠信於士大夫也。
sentences.literal: The appraisal says: General Li was earnest like a rustic man; his mouth could not bring forth eloquence; yet on the day of his death, those in the world who knew and who did not know all for him shed tears—his inner core truly won trust from gentlemen and great families.
sentences.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: Li Guang had the modest air of a countryman and seldom spoke well, yet the day he died everyone who had ever heard his name wept—for he had won the hearts of the gentry in truth.

sentences.chinese: 諺曰：「桃李不言，下自成蹊。」
sentences.literal: The proverb says, "Peach and plum do not speak—below them a path forms of itself."
sentences.idiomatic: The proverb runs: "The peach and the plum do not speak, yet a path forms beneath their branches."

sentences.chinese: 此言雖小，可以喻大。
sentences.literal: This saying, though small, can illustrate what is great.
sentences.idiomatic: A small saying may illumine a great truth.

sentences.chinese: 然三代之將，道家所忌，自廣至陵，遂亡其宗，哀哉！
sentences.literal: Yet generals of the three dynasties are what Daoists taboo; from Guang down to Ling, thus his clan was destroyed—alas!
sentences.idiomatic: Yet fate is said to turn against families that produce generation after generation of generals—from Li Guang to Li Ling the house of Li was destroyed. How bitter!

sentences.chinese: 孔子稱「志士仁人，有殺身以成仁，無求生以害仁」，「使於四方，不辱君命」，蘇武有之矣。
sentences.literal: Confucius called them "men of purpose and benevolent men—there are those who kill the body to complete benevolence, none who seek life and harm benevolence," and "sent to the four quarters, not disgracing the ruler's charge"—Su Wu had these.
sentences.idiomatic: Confucius said that the noble man may give his life to perfect humaneness but will not cling to life at its expense, and that an envoy must never shame his lord's commission—in Su Wu both sayings found their man.
